channel.title: NYT > World News
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: For Xi, Trump’s Embrace of War Proves China Needs More Power
item.description: China is learning lessons from the war in Iran. Chief among them, the United States may pose an even greater threat than Beijing thought.
item.dc:creator: David Pierson
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Xi Jinping, China’s top leader, during an annual legislative meeting in Beijing on Wednesday.

item.title: Surfers in Munich (Yes, Munich) Just Want Their Wave Back
item.description: For decades, enthusiasts rode the cresting Eisbach creek in the southern German city, some 200 miles from the nearest coast. But the wave has vanished, prompting arguments about how to restore it.
item.dc:creator: Christopher F. Schuetze and Laetitia Vançon
item.media:credit: Laetitia Vancon for The New York Times

item.title: New Airstrikes Batter Tehran
item.description: The Israeli military said it had sent a broad wave of strikes at the Iranian capital early Saturday. Some strikes hit an area near the Mehrabad Airport. Footage shared on state media showed multiple explosions and columns of smoke.
item.dc:creator: The New York Times

item.title: Lebanon at ‘Tipping Point’ as It Seeks to Disarm Hezbollah
item.description: The country is waiting to see if the government seizes on this moment to disarm the Iranian-backed armed group and how the militants will respond.
item.dc:creator: Christina Goldbaum
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Hezbollah supporters gathering in the southern suburb of Beirut, Lebanon, on Sunday to mourn the death of Iran’s supreme leader, Ayatollah Ali Khamenei.

item.title: He Had a Purple Heart, PTSD and a Rap Sheet. He Had to Leave the U.S.
item.description: After a long battle with drug addiction, Sae Joon Park felt settled in a new life. But he was deported last year and is now fighting to get back to Hawaii.
item.dc:creator: Max Kim
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Jean Chung for The New York Times
item.media:description: Sae Joon Park in his apartment in Busan, South Korea, in February. Despite living in the U.S. for nearly 50 years, Park was ordered to move back to South Korea in June 2025.

item.title: Jamil Jivani, a Friend of JD Vance, Channels Charlie Kirk in Canada
item.description: Jamil Jivani, a Conservative rookie member of Parliament, follows an American playbook to win over young men on college campuses.
item.dc:creator: Norimitsu Onishi
item.media:credit: Gavin John for The New York Times
item.media:description: Jamil Jivani, a conservative member of Parliament, talking with supporters after speaking at the University of Calgary last month.

item.title: Defense Without U.S. Help Is a Live Topic for Canada, Japan and Australia
item.description: The leaders of Japan and Canada are making a unified front on defense cooperation as President Trump raises the pressure over military spending.
item.dc:creator: Ian Austen
item.media:credit: Adrian Wyld/The Canadian Press, via Associated Press
item.media:description: Prime Minister Mark Carney of Canada arriving in Tokyo on Friday as part of a tour that also included Australia.

item.title: Iran’s Drone Attacks Plague the Gulf States
item.description: Missile launches from Iran have slowed, but relentless drone volleys could deplete air defenses in the region.
item.dc:creator: Lara Jakes
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Damage at an oil facility in Fujairah, United Arab Emirates, on Tuesday after the authorities said it had been hit by debris from an intercepted drone.

item.title: As Trump Out-Putins Putin, Russia’s Global Influence Erodes
item.description: The conflict in Iran may give Moscow a short-term boost economically and in Ukraine. But it has also shown the limits of Russia’s partnerships.
item.dc:creator: Paul Sonne
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: President Vladimir V. Putin of Russia went to a wreath-laying ceremony at the Tomb of the Unknown Soldier in Moscow last month.

item.title: ‘Liquor Capital of China’ Is Trying to Save Its Fortunes
item.description: Once celebrated for its fiery spirits, the town of Maotai has reeled from a bad Chinese economy, changing tastes and a crackdown on boozy official banquets.
item.dc:creator: Andrew Higgins and Gilles Sabrié
item.media:description: Tanks of fermented sorghum at Jinjiao Distillery in Maotai, China, in February.

item.title: Indonesia to Block Children Under 16 From Social Media
item.description: The ban is to take effect March 28, according to a government minister, but details about how it would be carried out were scarce.
item.dc:creator: Sui-Lee Wee
item.media:description: Children playing on smartphones in Yogyakarta, Indonesia, in January.

item.title: Iran’s Friends Include China and Russia. But Where are They Now?
item.description: Iran maintains ties with a range of countries, including Turkey, India, Russia and China. Yet in this war, their support is mostly rhetoric.
item.dc:creator: Ben Hubbard
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Arash Khamooshi for The New York Times
item.media:description: A police station that was destroyed by U.S.-Israeli airstrikes in Tehran.

item.title: South African President on Trump’s Oval Office ‘Ambush’ and ‘Racist’ Policies
item.description: In an interview with The New York Times, President Cyril Ramaphosa opened up about the role of middle powers, relations with Washington and apartheid.
item.dc:creator: John Eligon and Zimasa Matiwane
item.media:description: President Cyril Ramaphosa of South Africa opened up about his relationship with President Trump.

item.title: James Luckey-Lange Recounts Being Detained, Starved and Beaten in Venezuela
item.description: James Luckey-Lange, 28, wrote about kindness and shared humanity as he traveled. But he said he had been shackled, starved and beaten in Venezuela after being detained.
item.dc:creator: Annie Correal and Anatoly Kurmanaev
item.category: Goodbye Horses: The Many Lives of Q Lazzarus (Movie)
item.media:credit: Hannah Yoon for The New York Times
item.media:description: James Luckey-Lange was held by Venezuelan authorities for more than a month and was released after then President Nicolás Maduro was captured in January.

item.title: Israel Pushes Farther Into Lebanon, and Readies for More
item.description: Days into its offensive against Hezbollah, Israel is massing armored vehicles near the Lebanese border for a potentially much larger ground incursion.
item.dc:creator: David M. Halbfinger and Natan Odenheimer
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Avishag Shaar-Yashuv for The New York Times
item.media:description: Military trucks transporting tanks toward the Israeli-Lebanese border on Thursday.

item.title: After Gen-Z Revolution, Nepal Votes for a Fresh Slate of Leaders
item.description: A millennial rapper and former mayor is aiming to lead the country, six months after the old guard was ousted.
item.dc:creator: Hannah Beech, Binod Ghimire, Sajal Pradhan and Hannah Reyes Morales
item.media:description: Voting in Kathmandu, Nepal, on Thursday. The city still bears the scars of last year’s Gen Z-led revolution

item.title: Here’s the latest.
item.dc:creator: Farnaz Fassihi, Liam Stack and Max Kim

item.title: Airstrikes hit Tehran’s Mehrabad Airport.
item.dc:creator: Martín González Gómez
item.media:credit: Arash Khamooshi for The New York Times
item.media:description: Smoke billowing from multiple explosions near Mehrabad Airport in Tehran on Saturday.

item.title: State Department Bypasses Congress to Send Israel More Than 20,000 Bombs
item.description: The sale had been under informal review in Congress, but the State Department declared an emergency because of the war in Iran.
item.dc:creator: Edward Wong
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Arash Khamooshi for The New York Times
item.media:description: Multiple explosions near Mehrabad airport amid US-Israeli airstrikes in Tehran on Friday as part of their joint war on Iran.

item.title: Iran’s Navy Is Weakened but U.S. Still Faces Challenges in Strait of Hormuz
item.description: U.S. strikes have heavily targeted Iran’s large ships and bases, but its Revolutionary Guard navy is a more nimble force.
item.dc:creator: Christoph Koettl
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:description: The Port of Bandar Abbas in the Strait of Hormuz in 2023.

item.title: This is what happened on March 6.
Trump Echoes F.D.R. and Grant in Calling for ‘Unconditional Surrender’
item.description: President Trump demanded that Iran capitulate in the war with the United States, invoking a phrase made famous by statesmen and generals.
item.dc:creator: Neil Vigdor
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: President Trump declared on Friday that he would settle for nothing short of “unconditional surrender” by Iran.

item.title: Russia Gets Silent Treatment in Paralympics Return
item.description: Russia’s flag was paraded at the opening ceremony of the Paralympics for the first time since 2014 prompting a boycott by several nations led by Ukraine.
item.dc:creator: Tariq Panja and Josephine de La Bruyère
item.media:description: Two Russian athletes, center, and Russian officials participated in the opening ceremony of the Winter Paralympics in Verona, Italy.

item.title: Can we say senior UN official? he is the head of an agency
item.dc:creator: Farnaz Fassihi
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Sahar Al Attar/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: The Strait of Hormuz is a major maritime corridor for energy, food and other goods.

item.title: How the War in Iran Is Disrupting the World’s Oil
item.description: Our business reporter Peter Eavis looks at the global implications of the Strait of Hormuz, the narrow waterway to the south of Iran. He analyzes ship activity in the strait, comparing it before and after the United States and Israel initiated attacks on Iran.
item.dc:creator: Peter Eavis, Sutton Raphael, Leila Medina, Stephanie Swart, Blacki Migliozzi, Christiaan Triebert, Keith Collins, Jacqueline Gu and Rebecca F. Elliott
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)

item.title: How We Analyzed the Strike on the Iranian School
item.description: Malachy Browne of our Visual Investigations team describes what satellite imagery and other evidence tell us about who might be responsible for an airstrike on an elementary school in southern Iran. The strike killed at least 175 people, according to health officials and Iranian state media.
item.dc:creator: Malachy Browne, Aaron Boxerman, Coleman Lowndes and Estelle Caswell

item.title: Here Is Mexico’s Plan to Keep the Country Safe During the World Cup
item.description: Officials announced a massive deployment of security forces ahead of the world’s largest sporting event after recent violence prompted safety concerns for the millions of fans expected to attend.
item.dc:creator: James Wagner and David Shortell
item.media:description: Banorte Stadium, also known as Azteca Stadium, the venue in Mexico City that will host the opening match of the 2026 World Cup.

item.title: Pakistani Man Is Found Guilty of Plot to Kill Trump Backed by Iran
item.description: A federal jury in Brooklyn convicted Asif Merchant on Friday of planning to kill American politicians, in a scheme backed by the Iranian government. Mr. Merchant faces life in prison.
item.dc:creator: Santul Nerkar
item.media:credit: Dave Sanders for The New York Times
item.media:description: Asif Merchant was convicted of plotting to kill President Trump and other politicians in a plot backed by Iran on Friday.

item.title: Trump Demands Iran’s ‘Unconditional Surrender’ as Israel Pounds Tehran and Lebanon
item.description: Israel’s air forces shifted their focus back to Iran on Friday after a night of heavy bombardment in Lebanon, which was quickly becoming one of the largest fronts in the regional conflict.
item.dc:creator: David E. Sanger, Adam Rasgon, Euan Ward and Richard Pérez-Peña
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Arash Khamooshi for The New York Times
item.media:description: Explosions over west Tehran on Friday evening.

item.title: Macron speaks with Lebanon’s leader, as France seeks to broker a truce between Israel and Hezbollah.
item.dc:creator: Euan Ward, Natan Odenheimer and Catherine Porter
item.media:credit: Diego Ibarra Sanchez for The New York Times
item.media:description: Smoke rises after strikes on Beirut, Lebanon, on Thursday.

item.title: Israel says it struck an underground bunker at Khamenei’s compound.
item.dc:creator: Daniel Wood, Sanjana Varghese, Ronen Bergman and Euan Ward

item.title: As Trump Focuses on Cuba, DOJ Seeks Charges Against Its Leaders
item.description: The order could set the stage for a drive for regime change in Cuba, a goal President Trump has expressed.
item.dc:creator: Glenn Thrush, Alan Feuer, Luke Broadwater and Michael Crowley
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Jefferson Siegel for The New York Times
item.media:description: Jason A. Reding Quiñones, the United States attorney for the Southern District of Florida, is said to be looking to bring criminal charges against Cuba’s leaders.

item.title: Families Forced to Sleep on the Streets of Beirut as Israel Pounds Hezbollah
item.description: Thousands fled the southern outskirts of the Lebanese capital before Israel unleashed heavy strikes on the militant group overnight. Many say they have nowhere to go.
item.dc:creator: Abdi Latif Dahir
item.media:credit: Diego Ibarra Sanchez for The New York Times
item.media:description: Shefgar Othman is staying in a tent by Beirut’s seaside promenade with his family after they were told to evacuate their home in the city’s southern suburbs earlier this week.

item.title: Airspace in the region remains heavily restricted.
item.dc:creator: Lazaro Gamio and Zach Levitt

item.title: Israel Says It Struck Underground Bunker at Khamenei’s Compound
item.description: The Israeli military said the facility, under the site where Iran’s supreme leader was killed by a strike last week, was still being used after the first attack.
item.dc:creator: Sanjana Varghese, Ronen Bergman and Euan Ward
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)

item.title: New England Patriots’ Plane Ferried Americans Back From Middle East
item.description: The flight was arranged and paid for by the State Department, officials said, as part of the evacuations of citizens stranded by attacks in the Middle East.
item.dc:creator: Victor Mather and Michael Crowley
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Kirby Lee/Imagn Images, via Reuters Connect
item.media:description: A New England Patriots private plane in San Jose, Calif., last month. One of the team’s planes, operated by a charter company, was used to bring Americans home from the Middle East this week.

item.title: Trump Demands ‘Unconditional Surrender’ by Iran, Shifting U.S. Objectives Again
item.description: President Trump laid out yet another, more ambitious goal of U.S. military action, one that could extend the war.
item.dc:creator: David E. Sanger
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Arash Khamooshi for The New York Times
item.media:description: Iranians mourning the killing of Ayatollah Ali Khamenei, the supreme leader, in Tehran on Thursday night.

item.title: With Iran, Trump Takes the U.S. to War Without the Public’s Support
item.description: In opening a military campaign against Iran, President Trump is the first president in modern times to take the United States to war without the backing of the public.
item.dc:creator: Peter Baker
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:description: President Trump on the USS George H.W. Bush in October. Polling shows public support for striking Iran is below what his predecessors enjoyed initially when they used force overseas.

item.title: From 1978: Golda Meir, 80, Dies in Jerusalem; Israelis Acclaim ‘Stalwart Lioness’
item.description: Once a teacher in Milwaukee, she climbed the ranks of the Labor Party to become Israel’s first female prime minister.
item.dc:creator: Paul Hoffman and Special to The New York Times

item.title: From 2001: Beate Uhse, 81, Entrepreneur in the Business of Erotic Goods
item.description: She developed Europe’s biggest emporium of erotic goods and opened a three-story sex museum in Germany.
item.dc:creator: Wolfgang Saxon
item.media:description: Beate Uhse in 1971, in front of her mail-order company headquarters in Flensburg, Germany.

item.title: From 2011: Wangari Maathai, Nobel Peace Prize Laureate, Dies at 71
item.description: A Kenyan environmentalist, she began by paying women a few shillings to plant trees and went on to become the first African woman to win a Nobel Peace Prize.
item.dc:creator: Jeffrey Gettleman
item.media:description: Wangari Maathai in 2005, with a tree she planted in the Newlands Forest in Cape Town, South Africa.

item.title: Christine Keeler, Central Figure in British ‘Scandal of the Century,’ Is Dead at 75
item.description: News of her affair with the government minister John Profumo and other revelations set England abuzz in the early 1960s.
item.dc:creator: Neil Genzlinger

item.title: From 2007: Benazir Bhutto, 54, Who Weathered Pakistan’s Political Storm for 3 Decades, Dies
item.description: She was the first democratically elected woman to lead a modern Muslim country.
item.dc:creator: John F. Burns

item.title: From 1984: Indira Gandhi, Born to Politics, Left Her Own Imprint on India
item.description: The dominant figure in Indian politics for nearly two decades, she was elected prime minister four times before being assassinated by her bodyguards.
item.dc:creator: William K. Stevens and Special to The New York Times
item.media:description: Indira Gandhi in 1963. She served as India’s premier from 1966 to 1974, and was elected again in 1980.

item.title: From 2000: Sirimavo Bandaranaike of Sri Lanka Dies at 84; First Woman Premier
item.description: She rose to power in 1960 as a widow and mother of three, becoming the first woman in the world to serve as a prime minister.
item.dc:creator: Celia W. Dugger
item.media:description: Sirimavo Bandaranaike in 1961, speaking with Duncan Sandys, the British secretary of state for commonwealth relations.

item.title: From 1997: Mother Teresa, Hope of the Despairing, Dies at 87
item.description: She won the Nobel Peace Prize in 1979 for providing care and comfort to the impoverished.
item.dc:creator: Eric Pace
item.media:description: Mother Teresa around 1990. She set up welfare institutions, including a family clinic, mobile leprosy clinics and nurseries for abandoned children.

item.title: From 2013: Margaret Thatcher, ‘Iron Lady’ Who Set Britain on New Course, Dies at 87
item.description: She held the office of prime minister longer than any other British politician in the 20th century, setting her country on a right-leaning economic path.
item.dc:creator: Joseph R. Gregory
item.media:description: Margaret Thatcher in 1986. She was the first woman to become prime minister of Britain and the first to lead a major Western power in modern times.

item.title: From 2007: Vilma Espín, Rebel and Wife of Raúl Castro, Dies at 77
item.description: An idealistic socialite who fought alongside Fidel and Raúl Castro in the mountains of Cuba, she was the country’s unofficial first lady for decades.
item.dc:creator: Anthony DePalma
item.media:description: Vilma Espín around 1959. She became a widely recognized symbol of the Castro family’s half-century grip on power.

item.title: From 2019: Agnès Varda, Influential French New Wave Filmmaker, Dies at 90
item.description: She was closely associated with the film movement known as the New Wave, although her reimagining of cinematic conventions predated it.
item.dc:creator: John Anderson

item.title: From 1957: Gabriela Mistral, Poet, Is Dead; Won Nobel Prize for Literature
item.description: She was recognized in 1945 for three “Soñetos de la Muerte” (“Sonnets of Death”), which were first published in Chile in 1922.
item.dc:creator: Special to The New York Times
item.media:description: Gabriela Mistral, seated, in 1945, showing her Nobel Prize to the Swedish writer Hjalmar Gullberg, right.

item.title: From 2011: Nancy Wake, Proud Spy and Nazi Foe, Dies at 98
item.description: She underwent a wartime metamorphosis, from a fun-loving girl to a highly decorated Resistance fighter.
item.dc:creator: Paul Vitello
item.media:description: Nancy Wake in an undated photo. She received so many medals for her wartime service, she said, that she lived out her old age on the proceeds from their sale.

item.title: From 2011: Madame Nhu, Vietnam War Lightning Rod, Dies
item.description: The glamorous official hostess in South Vietnam’s presidential palace, she was a politically powerful and often outspoken figure during the Vietnam War.
item.dc:creator: Joseph R. Gregory
item.media:description: Madame Nhu in 1963. She was a forceful counterweight to her husband, Ngo Dinh Nhu, the chief political adviser and brother of former President Ngo Dinh Diem of South Vietnam.

item.title: From 1991: Suicide of Jiang Qing, Mao’s Widow, Is Reported
item.description: A former actress who grew up in poverty, she was one of the most hated women in modern Chinese history for her role in the Cultural Revolution.
item.dc:creator: Nicholas Kristof
item.media:credit: Sovfoto/Universal Images Group via Getty Images
item.media:description: Jiang Qing in 1945 with her husband, Mao Zedong, the chairman of the Chinese Communist Party. After he died in 1976, she soon lost power.

item.title: From 2003: Leni Riefenstahl, Filmmaker and Nazi Propagandist, Dies at 101
item.description: Her documentaries earned her acclaim as a cinematic genius, as well as criticism for putting her talent at the service of Hitler.
item.dc:creator: Alan Riding
item.media:credit: DB Walter Frentz Archiv/picture-alliance/dpa, via Associated Press
item.media:description: Leni Riefenstahl in 1936, the year that the German Olympic Committee commissioned her to record the Berlin Olympics.

item.title: Iranians flee to Turkey. ‘I am in constant fear,’ says one.
item.dc:creator: The New York Times
item.media:credit: Ali Ihsan Ozturk/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: Border crossings between Turkey and Iran have seen a steady stream of Iranian nationals fleeing the American and Israeli strikes in their country.

item.title: Israel issues an evacuation warning in the Qom region, near an Iranian nuclear site.
item.dc:creator: Euan Ward
item.media:description: A satellite image of the Fordo nuclear facility in Iran after it was struck by the United States in June.

item.title: Strikes Batter Iran’s Storied Azadi Stadium Complex
item.description: Several sports centers have been hit in Tehran since the U.S. and Israel began their attacks, the authorities say, but none with the resonance of the Azadi.
item.dc:creator: Erika Solomon
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Arash Khamooshi for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Azadi Indoor Complex in Tehran was struck on Thursday.

item.title: Trump Remarks on U.S.S. Cole and Iran Raise Questions in Guantánamo Case
item.description: Prosecutors have offered up no evidence supporting a link that the president claimed between Iran and the attack in Yemen in 2000, defense lawyers say.
item.dc:creator: Carol Rosenberg
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: The bombing of the U.S.S. Cole off the coast of Yemen was seen as a precursor of the Sept. 11 terrorist attacks.

item.title: Air travel remains disrupted across the Middle East.
item.dc:creator: Johnatan Reiss
item.media:description: Empty check-in counters previously occupied by Emirates and Etihad Airways at John F. Kennedy International Airport, in New York City on Monday.

item.title: This is what happened on March 5.
4 Arrested in London on Suspicion of Spying on Jews for Iran
item.description: The police said one Iranian and three British-Iranians were detained as part of a counterterrorism investigation.
item.dc:creator: Stephen Castle and Lizzie Dearden
item.media:description: The headquarters of the Metropolitan Police in London. The arrests were part of a long-running investigation, police said.

item.title: Khamenei’s Killing Sparks Anger and Grief in South Asia’s Shiite Muslims
item.description: Ayatollah Ali Khamenei, who was killed during the U.S.-Israeli strikes on Iran, was deeply respected among the millions of Shiite Muslims in Pakistan and India.
item.dc:creator: Showkat Nanda, Zia ur-Rehman, Suhasini Raj and Max Kim
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Shiite Muslims during a candlelit protest in Srinagar on Wednesday.

item.title: 3 Australians Were on U.S. Submarine That Sank an Iranian Warship
item.description: Prime Minister Anthony Albanese’s disclosure about the attack in the Indian Ocean prompted concern that Australia’s close military cooperation with the United States could draw it into the fighting in the Middle East.
item.dc:creator: Victoria Kim
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:description: An injured Iranian sailor is moved on a stretcher at Galle National Hospital in Sri Lanka after the torpedoing of the Iranian naval ship IRIS Dena on Thursday.
